refined_fr.refined_fr: The NGHE solution shall achieve a measured p95 cycle time ≤ CT_target for the load→travel→dump cycle under the agreed duty cycle and site conditions, as verified through on-site performance testing.
refined_fr.rationale: CR-NGHE-002 is a key performance indicator (KPI) requirement that directly impacts customer productivity and ROI. Refinement adds measurable verification criteria (p95 percentile, duty cycle context, site conditions) and clarifies the scope boundary: the full load-travel-dump cycle is the unit of measurement. This prevents ambiguity about which sub-cycles are included and establishes the statistical rigor (p95, not mean) required for acceptance.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Instrumented on-site testing under representative duty cycle; telemetry capture of cycle timestamps; statistical analysis of ≥100 cycles to establish p95 confidence; comparison against CT_target baseline.
sub_frs: FR-2.1: The vehicle shall minimize load acquisition time through optimized bucket fill and positioning logic.
FR-2.2: The vehicle shall minimize travel time through route optimization and speed management within safety constraints.
FR-2.3: The vehicle shall minimize dump time through efficient bucket positioning and material release.
FR-2.4: The teleoperation system shall introduce ≤ acceptable_latency_budget to cycle time under nominal network conditions.
FR-2.5: The safety services shall not impose cycle time penalties beyond the agreed safety margin budget.

candidate_dps: DP-2.A: Implement real-time cycle time monitoring and predictive analytics to identify bottlenecks; optimize vehicle control algorithms (bucket fill, travel speed, dump sequencing) based on duty cycle profiling; allocate latency budgets across teleoperation and safety subsystems.

DP-2.B: Deploy a hierarchical control architecture with local autonomy (vehicle-level cycle optimization) and remote oversight (tele-operator monitoring); use network-aware scheduling to minimize latency impact on cycle time.

DP-2.C: Establish a performance baseline through instrumented testing; implement adaptive control tuning based on site-specific conditions (terrain, material properties, operator skill); use safety-aware speed governors to enforce cycle time vs. safety trade-offs.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP-2.A: Implement real-time cycle time monitoring and predictive analytics to identify bottlenecks; optimize vehicle control algorithms (bucket fill, travel speed, dump sequencing) based on duty cycle profiling; allocate latency budgets across teleoperation and safety subsystems.

potential_sources: Coupling between vehicle control optimization (load/travel/dump) and teleoperation latency budget: both affect cycle time but are independently tunable.
Coupling between safety services and cycle time: safety constraints (speed governors, collision avoidance) may limit optimization headroom; mitigated by explicit safety margin budget allocation.
Coupling between duty cycle profiling and site-specific conditions: profiling must be representative; risk mitigated by on-site baseline testing.
coupling_check.notes: DP-2.A decouples the problem into three semi-independent optimization domains (vehicle algorithms, teleoperation latency, safety margins) with explicit budget allocation. This is preferable to DP-2.B (which conflates local autonomy with remote oversight) and DP-2.C (which is reactive rather than proactive). Coupling is manageable via interface contracts (latency SLAs, safety margin budgets).

assumptions_risks: Assumption: Cycle time bottlenecks are identifiable and tunable via control algorithm optimization; Risk: If bottleneck is inherent to vehicle kinematics or site terrain, optimization headroom may be insufficient.
Assumption: Teleoperation latency can be bounded and budgeted; Risk: Network variability or congestion may violate latency SLA, requiring fallback to local autonomy or cycle time relaxation.
Assumption: Safety margin budget is achievable without unacceptable cycle time penalty; Risk: Safety constraints (e.g., collision avoidance, speed limits) may consume more budget than allocated.
Assumption: Duty cycle profiling is representative of actual customer operations; Risk: Site-specific variations (material type, terrain, operator skill) may invalidate profiling assumptions.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: Refine DP-2.A to explicitly define: (1) cycle time monitoring instrumentation and telemetry schema, (2) bottleneck identification methodology (e.g., statistical analysis of sub-cycle times), (3) control algorithm tuning parameters and optimization bounds, (4) latency budget allocation strategy (e.g., percentile-based SLA), (5) safety margin budget quantification and trade-off decision rules. This will enable clearer IM assignment and gate review.

im_boundary.name: Vehicle
im_boundary.scope: The Vehicle module owns the physical and control-level realization of cycle time optimization: bucket fill logic, travel speed management, dump sequencing, and local sensor integration. It receives Tasking commands (cycle objectives) and Telemetry requests; it emits Telemetry (cycle time metrics, sub-cycle timestamps, bottleneck indicators) and SafetyAlert (if cycle time optimization conflicts with safety constraints).

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Receives cycle objectives (load location, travel destination, dump site) and performance targets (CT_target, duty cycle parameters) from NGHE Solution or Teleoperation.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Emits real-time cycle time metrics: sub-cycle timestamps (load start/end, travel start/end, dump start/end), bottleneck indicators, control algorithm state, and p95 cycle time statistics.

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Signals when cycle time optimization conflicts with safety constraints (e.g., speed governor limiting travel speed, collision avoidance forcing detour).

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Communicates with Teleoperation and Safety Services for remote monitoring, tele-operator commands, and safety constraint updates; latency on this link directly impacts cycle time.

module_containment_scores.notes: Vehicle module is the natural owner of cycle time optimization logic: bucket fill, travel speed, dump sequencing are all vehicle-level control functions. Telemetry emission is contained. Risk of diffusion: latency budget allocation and safety margin trade-offs may require coordination with Teleoperation and Safety Services; mitigated by explicit interface contracts.

Teleoperation could own latency budget management and remote cycle time monitoring, but cycle time optimization logic (bucket fill, travel speed, dump sequencing) is inherently vehicle-level. Assigning to Teleoperation would diffuse control logic across two modules and create tight coupling.

Safety Services owns safety constraint enforcement (speed governors, collision avoidance), which impacts cycle time. However, cycle time optimization is not a safety function; assigning to Safety Services would conflate safety and performance concerns and create diffusion.

NGHE Solution could own cycle time orchestration and KPI tracking at the system level, but detailed control algorithm optimization is vehicle-level. Assigning to NGHE Solution would create a high-level coordination layer but still require Vehicle to implement the actual optimization logic.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: The Vehicle module is the primary owner of cycle time optimization (DP-2.A). It implements real-time monitoring, bottleneck identification, and control algorithm tuning. Teleoperation and Safety Services are secondary stakeholders: Teleoperation manages latency budget (via Network Link interface), and Safety Services enforces safety margin budget (via SafetyAlert interface). NGHE Solution coordinates KPI tracking and duty cycle profiling at the system level.
containment_risks: Risk: Latency budget allocation requires coordination between Vehicle and Teleoperation; if not explicitly managed via interface contract, latency SLA violations could cascade into cycle time failures.

Risk: Safety margin budget trade-offs require coordination between Vehicle and Safety Services; if not explicitly managed, safety constraints could consume more budget than allocated, invalidating cycle time target.
Risk: Duty cycle profiling and site-specific tuning require coordination between Vehicle and NGHE Solution; if profiling is not representative, on-site performance may not meet CT_target.
Risk: Telemetry instrumentation and bottleneck identification methodology must be consistent across Vehicle and NGHE Solution; if not, KPI verification may be ambiguous.
assigned_im.recommended_revision: Establish explicit interface contracts: (1) Tasking interface: define cycle objective schema and CT_target parameter format. (2) Telemetry interface: define cycle time metric schema (sub-cycle timestamps, p95 statistics, bottleneck indicators). (3) SafetyAlert interface: define conflict signals (speed governor active, collision avoidance active, etc.). (4) Network Link: define latency SLA (e.g., p95 latency ≤ X ms) and fallback behavior if SLA is violated. (5) Duty cycle profiling: define profiling methodology and site-specific tuning procedure. These contracts will enable clear ownership boundaries and risk mitigation.

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP-2.A directly addresses the refined FR: real-time monitoring and predictive analytics enable identification of cycle time bottlenecks; control algorithm optimization targets the three sub-cycles (load, travel, dump); latency budget allocation ensures teleoperation and safety constraints are managed. The DP is solution-neutral (does not prescribe specific algorithms or hardware) and is verifiable through on-site testing.

diffusion_risks: Latency budget allocation spans Vehicle and Teleoperation modules; mitigated by explicit Network Link interface contract.
Safety margin budget trade-offs span Vehicle and Safety Services modules; mitigated by explicit SafetyAlert interface contract.
Duty cycle profiling and site-specific tuning span Vehicle and NGHE Solution modules; mitigated by explicit Tasking interface contract and profiling methodology documentation.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: Vehicle module is the primary owner of cycle time optimization logic (bucket fill, travel speed, dump sequencing). Telemetry emission is contained within Vehicle. Latency and safety margin budgets are managed via external interfaces (Network Link, SafetyAlert) with clear ownership boundaries. Diffusion is minimized by explicit interface contracts.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: Cycle time KPI is directly measurable via Telemetry interface (sub-cycle timestamps, p95 statistics). On-site testing under representative duty cycle is feasible. Bottleneck identification is verifiable through telemetry analysis. Control algorithm tuning is verifiable through iterative testing and performance comparison against CT_target.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: Vehicle module owns cycle time optimization logic and telemetry emission. Teleoperation module owns latency budget management (via Network Link). Safety Services module owns safety margin budget enforcement (via SafetyAlert). NGHE Solution module owns system-level KPI tracking and duty cycle profiling. Ownership boundaries are clear and non-overlapping.

recommended_revisions: Refine Tasking interface to include CT_target and duty cycle parameters (material type, terrain profile, operator skill level).
Refine Telemetry interface to include sub-cycle timestamps (load start/end, travel start/end, dump start/end), bottleneck indicators, and p95 statistics.
Refine SafetyAlert interface to include conflict signals (speed governor active, collision avoidance active, etc.) and impact on cycle time.
Define Network Link latency SLA (e.g., p95 latency ≤ X ms) and fallback behavior if SLA is violated.
Document duty cycle profiling methodology and site-specific tuning procedure.
Establish acceptance criteria for cycle time KPI verification (e.g., ≥100 cycles, p95 ≤ CT_target, under agreed duty cycle and site conditions).

decision.justification: The refined FR is well-defined and measurable; DP-2.A is a sound solution concept with manageable coupling; Vehicle module is the appropriate owner with clear interface contracts. However, DP-2.A is not atomic: it encompasses three semi-independent optimization domains (vehicle control algorithms, teleoperation latency budget, safety margin budget) that require further decomposition to enable detailed design and implementation. Decomposing into sub-FRs will clarify ownership boundaries, interface contracts, and verification strategies for each domain.
next_level_sub_frs: FR-2.1: The vehicle shall minimize load acquisition time through optimized bucket fill and positioning logic.
FR-2.2: The vehicle shall minimize travel time through route optimization and speed management within safety constraints.
FR-2.3: The vehicle shall minimize dump time through efficient bucket positioning and material release.
FR-2.4: The teleoperation system shall introduce ≤ acceptable_latency_budget to cycle time under nominal network conditions.
FR-2.5: The safety services shall not impose cycle time penalties beyond the agreed safety margin budget.

assumptions: Cycle time bottlenecks are identifiable and tunable via control algorithm optimization.
Teleoperation latency can be bounded and budgeted via explicit SLA.
Safety margin budget is achievable without unacceptable cycle time penalty.
Duty cycle profiling is representative of actual customer operations.
Vehicle module has sufficient computational and control authority to implement cycle time optimization.
Telemetry instrumentation is available and can be integrated without significant cost or complexity.
On-site testing is feasible and representative of customer operations.
open_risks: Latency SLA violations due to network congestion or variability; mitigation: define fallback behavior (e.g., local autonomy, cycle time relaxation).
Safety constraints consuming more budget than allocated; mitigation: establish safety margin budget trade-off decision rules.
Site-specific variations invalidating duty cycle profiling; mitigation: implement on-site tuning procedure and baseline testing.
Control algorithm optimization reaching diminishing returns; mitigation: establish optimization bounds and fallback strategies.
Telemetry instrumentation introducing unacceptable overhead; mitigation: optimize telemetry schema and sampling rates.
evidence_needed: Duty cycle profiling data (material type, terrain profile, operator skill level, historical cycle times).
Network latency characterization (nominal, p95, p99 latencies under representative load).
Safety constraint impact analysis (speed governor limits, collision avoidance detours, cycle time penalties).
Vehicle control algorithm baseline (current bucket fill, travel speed, dump sequencing logic).
Telemetry instrumentation design (sub-cycle timestamp capture, bottleneck identification methodology).
On-site testing plan (test duration, duty cycle representation, acceptance criteria).
Acceptance criteria for cycle time KPI (p95 ≤ CT_target, confidence level, duty cycle context).
